title: Proverbs 15
subtitle: Gentle Answer; Eyes of the LORD; Better Than; Path of Life Leads Upward

sections.header: Verses 1–17 — Gentle Answer; Eyes of the LORD; Better Than Wealth

heb.paragraph: A rak (gentle, soft) answer turns away wrath (v. 1). The adjective describes something yielding and tender — the opposite of the ʿeṣem (harsh word) that stirs up anger. The proverb is not about weakness but about strategic de-escalation: softness disarms aggression more effectively than matching force.

The eyes of the LORD are in every place, keeping watch on the evil and the good (v. 3). The image is of comprehensive divine surveillance — not paranoid but pastoral. Nothing escapes notice. This grounds all of Proverbs' moral teaching in theology: behaviour matters because God sees.

refs.note: The tongue of the wise brings healing -- the soothing tongue as tree of life (15:4) extends this.
Quick to listen, slow to speak, slow to anger -- NT application of v.1's conflict resolution.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The speech-ethics cluster that opens ch.15 is its most concentrated. Alter notes the opening proverb (gentle answer turns away wrath) is one of the most practically useful observations in the entire collection — it is immediately actionable, universally applicable, and almost certainly true by direct experience. The cluster that follows (tongue of the wise brings knowledge; tongue of fools pours out folly) develops what the first proverb implies: speech shapes the relational environment.

'Death and Destruction lie open before the LORD — how much more do human hearts!' The argument a fortiori grounds divine omniscience: if Sheol and Abaddon (Death and Destruction) are visible to God, then certainly the human heart — which is less hidden than the realm of the dead — is fully transparent to him. Alter notes the underground/underwater realm of Sheol is the most inaccessible possible place; and even that is visible.

waltke.source: Bruce K. Waltke, NICOT Commentary on Proverbs (2 vols.) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The gentle-answer opening is one of Proverbs' most practical observations about social conflict: 'a gentle answer turns away wrath, but a harsh word stirs up anger.' Waltke notes the speech-control proverb group of vv.1–7 establishes that wisdom in conflict is primarily a speech discipline — not avoidance of conflict but management of its escalation through deliberate word-choice.

'The eyes of the LORD are everywhere, keeping watch on the wicked and the good.' This is the theological centre of ch.15. Waltke notes divine omniscience is here presented not primarily as a threat but as a structural reality that grounds all the other observations of the chapter. Every speech act, every household meal, every route of life — all are in the presence of the God who sees.

Calvin: 'A soft answer turns away wrath — this is one of those practical proverbs that needs no commentary to be understood. Experience teaches everyone that anger is disarmed by gentleness. But to put the principle into practice in the moment of provocation requires exactly the character formation that Proverbs is building.'

Calvin on divine omniscience: 'If God's eyes penetrate even to the depths of hell, how much more must they see the innermost recesses of our hearts? This thought should both encourage and constrain us: encourage, because no suffering is beyond his sight; constrain, because no concealed sin is beyond his knowledge.'

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on 'a gentle answer turns away wrath' (ma'aneh rakh yashuv chemah) discusses ma'aneh (a response, an answer) — the proverb addresses what you say in reply to an angry person, not how you initiate conversation. The gentle response is a social skill that de-escalates; the harsh word (davar etsev, a painful/grievous word) escalates. The note identifies this as one of Proverbs' most-cited practical observations.

The NET note on 'the eyes of the LORD are everywhere' (bekol-makom einei YHWH) discusses the theological significance of omniscience in the wisdom tradition: God's oversight is not primarily surveillance for punishment but the structural reality that all human behaviour occurs in the presence of the evaluating God. The note connects to Prov 5:21 ('a man's ways are in full view of the LORD') as the same point from a different angle.

hist.context: The first half is dominated by the tongue (vv.1-2,4,7) and God's omniscience (vv.3,8-9,11). The eyes of the LORD everywhere (v.3) and Death open before him (v.11) are the theological environment in which all speech ethics operate. The two better-than sayings (vv.16-17) introduce the theme: quality of heart matters more than quantity of possession.

sections.header: Verses 18–33 — Path of Life Leads Upward; LORD Hears the Righteous; Humility Before Honour

heb.paragraph: The path of life (ʾōraḥ ḥayyîm) leads upward for the prudent, to keep them from going down to Sheol (v. 24). The spatial metaphor — upward vs. downward — maps moral reality onto physical direction. Wisdom is an ascent; folly is a descent. The imagery anticipates later biblical theology of resurrection and afterlife.

Before honour is ʿănāwâ — humility (v. 33). This is not self-deprecation but accurate self-assessment before God. The sequence matters: humility is the precondition, not the result, of honour. The fear of the LORD is the mûsār (discipline/instruction) of wisdom, and humility comes before honour — the verse ties together the book's three great themes.

refs.note: He gives grace to the humble -- the honour-through-humility sequence of v.33, cited by James (4:6) and Peter (1 Pet 5:5).
Many advisers (v.22) -- the repeated counsellor-proverb. Wisdom is consistently communal.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The 'better than' proverbs (vv.16–17) are among the most counter-cultural in Proverbs. Alter notes their structure: each offers a comparison that challenges the default assumption (wealth/abundance = better). 'Better a little with the fear of the LORD than great wealth with turmoil' directly challenges the logic of the prosperity assumption. 'Better a small serving of vegetables with love than a fattened calf with hatred' challenges the assumption that material abundance trumps relational health.

'The heart of the righteous weighs its answers, but the mouth of the wicked gushes evil.' Alter notes the contrast of deliberation vs. gushing: the righteous person's speech is preceded by inner reflection (weighs); the wicked person's mouth just pours out whatever is there. The physiology of the two speeches — weighing vs. gushing — encodes their moral difference.

waltke.source: Bruce K. Waltke, NICOT Commentary on Proverbs (2 vols.) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The second section develops the 'better than' proverbs (vv.16–17) and the path-of-life imagery. Waltke notes the 'better than' form is Proverbs' most explicit value-clarification device: it forces a ranking between two goods or two evils. 'Better a little with the fear of the LORD than great wealth with turmoil' (v.16) is a direct challenge to the prosperity assumption — the fear of the LORD is more valuable than wealth.

'Wisdom's instruction is to fear the LORD, and humility comes before honor.' The chapter closes with the thesis statement framed as a journey: fear of the LORD → humility → honour. The sequence is the wisdom life's arc: the foundational orientation (fear of the LORD) produces the character quality (humility) that produces the social outcome (honour). The book's thesis appears again at the chapter's end.

Calvin on 'better than' proverbs: 'These comparisons teach us to subordinate external prosperity to spiritual good. A poor man who fears God is richer than a Croesus who has forgotten him. This is not merely an abstract theological claim — it is a practical observation about where genuine wellbeing actually resides.'

Calvin: 'The instruction of wisdom is to fear the LORD — and humility is both the fruit of this fear and the gate through which wisdom enters. The proud cannot be taught because they already think they know; the humble can be taught because they acknowledge they do not. Humility is not self-deprecation but accurate self-knowledge.'

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on the 'better than' form (tov...min) identifies this as one of Proverbs' most important rhetorical structures — explicit value-ranking that forces comparative judgment. The notes trace the 'better than' structure through Proverbs (15:16–17; 16:8, 19, 32; 17:1; 19:1; 21:9, 19; 25:7, 24; 27:5, 10; 28:6) as a sustained curriculum in value-clarification for the wisdom student.

The NET note on 'humility comes before honour' (lifney khavod anavah) discusses the Hebrew word order: 'before honour [is] humility.' The note traces this principle through 18:12 (before destruction the heart is haughty; before honour comes humility) and into the NT (Jas 4:10; 1 Pet 5:6). The principle is consistent across the entire wisdom tradition: genuine honour is the consequence of genuine humility, not its substitute.

hist.context: The second half continues the speech theme (vv.23,26,28) before addressing the path of life (v.24), the LORD's care for the vulnerable widow (v.25), the LORD's proximity to the righteous (v.29), and the chapter's closing sequence: wisdom → fear → humility → honour (v.33).

ppl.name: The gentle person
ppl.text: Speech that redirects rather than amplifies anger.

ppl.name: The widow
ppl.text: The most vulnerable under direct divine protection.

ppl.name: The humble
ppl.text: The sequence: fear of LORD → humility → honour.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIV"A gentle answer turns away wrath, but a harsh word stirs up anger."NoteThe most immediately practical verse in Proverbs 15. Gentleness is not weakness but the redirection of force.

src.title: Sheol and Abaddon
src.quote: Sheol (the grave) and Abaddon (Destruction). Both are open to God's sight -- the argument from the extreme (death itself) to the proximate (human hearts).
src.note: The merism encompasses the totality of the hidden realm of death.

src.title: Widow's boundary stones
src.quote: Ancient land markers strictly protected by Mosaic law (Deut 19:14). Widows were particularly vulnerable to encroachment.
src.note: The vulnerability of widows is addressed repeatedly in biblical law and wisdom.

rec.text: A gentle answer turns away wrath -- the most cited Proverbs verse in pastoral and counselling literature on communication.
James 4:6 and 1 Peter 5:5 quote Proverbs 3:34 ('God opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble'), not 15:33

rows.label: The Soft Answer
rows.text: A gentle answer turns away wrath, but a harsh word stirs up anger. One of Proverbs' most practically precise observations: the register of the response, not just its content, determines the interaction's direction.

rows.label: Better a Little
rows.text: Better a little with the fear of the LORD than great wealth with turmoil. The two better-than proverbs establish the priority: the fear of the LORD and love produce a quality of life that no quantity of wealth can replicate.

tx.title: Proverbs 15 — LXX additions and omissions
tx.content: The LXX adds several proverbs to chapter 15 not found in MT and omits at least one MT proverb. The additions tend to be more overtly theological, mentioning God and divine judgment more frequently than MT's equivalents. The overall chapter is 10–15% longer in LXX.
tx.note: The LXX additions to Proverbs 15 are among the clearest evidence that the LXX Proverbs translator was working with editorial freedom — adding material from other collections or composing independently.

debate.title: The 'Better Than' Proverbs and Prosperity Theology

positions.name: A direct refutation of material prosperity as the supreme good
positions.argument: The 'better than' proverbs (15:16–17; 16:8, 19) explicitly subordinate material wealth to the fear of the LORD, love, and righteousness. They are one of Proverbs' most direct challenges to any theology that identifies material blessing as the supreme indicator of God's favour.

positions.name: A qualification, not a rejection of prosperity
positions.argument: The 'better than' proverbs qualify the prosperity principle (if you must choose, choose righteousness over wealth) without rejecting the general principle that wisdom leads to material blessing. They are the exception clauses, not the rule.

positions.name: A realistic wisdom about competing goods
positions.argument: The 'better than' proverbs teach the wisdom student to rank competing goods correctly. They do not reject material prosperity as a genuine good — they insist that spiritual and relational goods are more valuable when they conflict with material ones. The ranking exercise is the wisdom pedagogy itself.

prayer_prompt: A gentle answer turns away wrath, but a harsh word stirs up anger. Consider asking God about your words in conflict — gentle or harsh, and what that choice costs.

coaching.tip: 'A gentle answer turns away wrath, but a harsh word stirs up anger' (v. 1). The chapter opens with interpersonal dynamics — how you speak determines what you receive back. Gentleness de-escalates; harshness inflames. This isn't weakness but wisdom. The wise person controls conversations by controlling their own tone.

'The eyes of the LORD are everywhere, keeping watch on the wicked and the good' (v. 3). Divine omniscience isn't just theology but practical reality. Nothing is hidden. This awareness should shape behavior — not from fear alone but from recognition that all of life is lived before an audience of One.
